reasoning: The inverted ternary bug at lines 160 and 203 of customizer.php is a confirmed functional defect — `isset($setting_props['default']) ? '' : $setting_props['default']` always returns '' when the key exists (the normal case) rather than the actual default array. However, it is not a security vulnerability for several concrete reasons: (1) Sanitization callbacks operate on user-submitted values, not on the WP_Customize_Setting default. The default is only a fallback when no saved value exists; it does not gate or bypass sanitize_callback execution. (2) get_setting_sanitization_callback() returns false (no sanitization) for the top-level font setting — this pre-existed the bug and is intentional per code comment ('sanitization happens on subsetting level'). Prop-level callbacks (esc_attr, sanitize_color, sanitize_unit, etc.) run on submitted values regardless of the incorrect default. (3) Access is gated to edit_theme_options capability (Administrator-level). There is no unauthenticated or subscriber-level path to this code. (4) The setting_id is matched against a strict alphanumeric regex before any processing occurs, preventing injection via the setting ID itself. (5) No attacker-controlled value flows from the incorrect empty-string default to an XSS sink or stored injection point accessible to lower-privileged users. The net effect is a UX/functional regression: font settings in the Customizer may not render correct theme defaults, but there is no security-relevant impact. The finding's classification as a security finding is not supported by the actual code path.
